does: GDPval introduces a benchmark of real-world economically valuable tasks spanning 44 occupations across nine major U.S. GDP-contributing sectors, constructed from authentic work of experienced industry professionals. The benchmark covers the majority of work activities from the Bureau of Labor Statistics taxonomy. The authors evaluate frontier AI models on these tasks and find performance improving roughly linearly over time, with current best models approaching expert-level deliverable quality. They analyze cost and speed advantages when pairing frontier models with human oversight and demonstrate that reasoning effort, task context, and scaffolding systematically improve model performance. The work includes open-sourcing 220 gold-standard tasks and providing public automated grading infrastructure to enable reproducible evaluation research.
connects: This work extends a line of research evaluating AI capabilities on practical benchmarks beyond academic datasets, building on efforts like MMLU and real-world task evaluations. It connects to broader work on measuring AI economic impact and labor market displacement potential, addressing gaps in how benchmarks capture authentic professional work. The paper builds on previous findings that frontier models perform surprisingly well on specialized domains when given proper context and scaffolding, extending these insights across diverse occupational contexts. It responds to limitations in existing capability benchmarks by grounding evaluation in actual industry standards and deliverable quality rather than narrow academic metrics, and incorporates insights from human-in-the-loop AI system design about effective collaboration structures.
enables: GDPval provides a foundation for ongoing evaluation of AI capabilities as models scale and improve, enabling rigorous tracking of when and how AI systems approach human expert performance across economic sectors. The public evaluation infrastructure facilitates future research on which occupations and tasks are most susceptible to AI augmentation versus automation, informing policy discussions about economic disruption and labor market transitions. The benchmark enables research into optimal human-AI collaboration structures by establishing baselines for supervised augmentation scenarios. It opens directions for studying which task characteristics and domain complexities most resist AI performance, potentially revealing fundamental limitations in current approaches, and provides measurement apparatus for evaluating emerging techniques like improved reasoning, better prompting, and contextual understanding on economically meaningful outcomes rather than academic metrics alone.
